note: Medians across n runs per cell (tree/graph n=5, flat n=3), one process per run, single machine. Headline metric is runtime.MemStats.GCCPUFraction — the share of total process CPU spent in GC since process start. Absolute percentages are machine- and workload-specific; the durable claims are the DIRECTION and the HEAP-SHAPE DEPENDENCE, not the exact -77%.

method.ab_mechanism: Same source, one toolchain (go1.26.5), compiled twice: default build = Green Tea GC (the 1.26 default), GOEXPERIMENT=nogreenteagc build = pre-1.26 classic GC.

shapes.tree: full ternary tree of 32-byte nodes (3 pointers + int64) — pointer-dense, high fanout
shapes.graph: n small nodes each pointing at 3 random others — pointer-dense with cross-object edges (worst case for a scanning GC)
shapes.flat: pointer-free int64 slabs (1M int64 = 8MB each) — almost nothing to scan

method.load: live heap ~400MB of the chosen shape, then `churn` iterations allocating+dropping quarter-sized garbage of the same shape, to force GC cycles over that live heap. This is deliberately the 'heavily uses the GC' regime.
method.pinned: GOGC=100 via debug.SetGCPercent(100) in both builds; fixed sizes; PRNG seeded 42

metrics.label: GC overhead (GCCPUFraction, median) — classic GC vs Green Tea GC, per heap shape
metrics.unit: % of process CPU spent in GC
metrics.caveat: flat's relative change is arithmetically similar to tree/graph but meaningless in practice: both sides are already under 0.5% of CPU, and flat's churn is only ~200ms so GCCPUFraction (a since-start cumulative) is noisy there — per-run values spanned 0.14%-0.75% (classic) and 0.06%-0.43% (green).

metrics.label: Wall time of the churn phase (median ms) — the practical consequence
metrics.pattern: The GC-CPU win converts to wall time ONLY where GC was actually a meaningful share of CPU. flat's GC overhead was already ~0.3%, so a 73% cut of it moves nothing.

metrics.label: Number of GC cycles during the measured churn (per-run values)
metrics.pattern: Cycle counts are essentially unchanged (35->31, 15->14, 12->12). The saving is not 'fewer collections' — it is cheaper marking/scanning PER cycle, which is exactly what Green Tea's design targets.

findings.claim: On pointer-dense heaps, Green Tea cut GC CPU by 70-77% and that showed up as a 14-29% shorter run.
findings.detail: tree: 10.30% -> 2.38% GC CPU, 4772.6ms -> 3377.3ms wall. graph: 5.72% -> 1.72%, 7540.0ms -> 6506.4ms. Both are medians over n=5.

findings.claim: On a pointer-free heap the same toolchain change is a no-op in practice.
findings.detail: flat: GC CPU 0.34% -> 0.09% (a real ratio, an irrelevant magnitude) and wall time 201.9ms -> 200.8ms (-0.5%, inside noise). Green Tea improves SCANNING; with almost no pointers to scan there is nothing to improve.

findings.claim: The mechanism visible here is cheaper cycles, not fewer cycles.
findings.detail: GC counts moved 35->31 / 15->14 / 12->12 while GC CPU fell ~3-4x. Note this is the OPPOSITE direction from DoltHub's 2025-09 report, which described per-GC CPU going UP and total GC count going DOWN (netting out to neutral) on their workload — a workload/version difference we did not reproduce or refute here.

findings.claim: These percentages are larger than what a real service should expect.
findings.detail: The harness does nothing but allocate and drop, so GC is an outsized share of its CPU. The Go team's own guidance is 10-40% GC-overhead reduction in GC-heavy programs, with additional ~10% from vectorized scanning on newer amd64. Our -77% is the same phenomenon amplified by an extreme regime, not a promise about production.

findings.claim: Green Tea is the default in Go 1.26 and the opt-out is scheduled to disappear.
findings.detail: Disabling is build-time only: GOEXPERIMENT=nogreenteagc. The Go 1.26 release notes state it 'is expected to be removed in Go 1.27' — i.e. this exact A/B becomes unbuildable on 1.27+.

explicitly_not_verified.note: Windows/amd64 only. Linux servers, macOS, and ARM64 were NOT measured. Green Tea's vectorized scan path is amd64-specific (Ice Lake / Zen 4+); ARM64 behavior is unknown to this run.

Measured on a Zen 5 part WITH AVX-512, i.e. the best case for the vectorized scanner. We did not measure a pre-AVX-512 amd64 machine, so we cannot say how much of the win is vectorization vs the new scan algorithm.

PauseTotalNs was recorded but is dominated by sub-10ms noise at this scale (0-9ms totals) and no pause DISTRIBUTION or tail-latency measurement was done. Claims about GC pause behavior are cited, not measured.
Go 1.26 also reports ~30% lower cgo call overhead. Out of scope — not exercised by this harness.
One operating point only: ~400MB live, GOGC=100. No sweep over heap size or GOGC, so we cannot say how the win scales with heap size.
No real application was benchmarked. tile38's reported ~35% and DoltHub's reported neutral result are cited from their write-ups, not reproduced here.
